title: What to do when your fixed energy deal ends
description: What happens when your fixed energy deal ends, your 49-day exit-fee-free window, and how to compare new fixed quotes against the Ofgem price cap.
blurb: What actually happens when your fix ends, the 49-day exit-fee-free window, and how to pick your next tariff.
lede: A fixed energy deal ending is one of the few moments in the year when ten minutes of attention reliably saves real money. Do nothing and your supplier quietly rolls you onto its standard variable tariff, priced under the Ofgem cap, and your monthly payments often change at the same time. Do a little homework in the final weeks of your fix and you can leave early without penalty, compare new deals against the cap with two numbers from your bill, and land on a tariff that actually suits how you use energy. This guide walks through exactly what happens, what your rights are, and how to decide between fixing again, riding the variable rate, or moving to a time-of-use tariff.
short_answer: If you do nothing when your fixed deal ends, you move onto your supplier's standard variable tariff, which is priced under the Ofgem cap: 26.11p per kWh for electricity and 7.33p for gas from July to September 2026, about £1,863 a year for a typical dual-fuel home. In the last 49 days of your fix you can switch to any supplier without paying exit fees. So compare fixed quotes against the cap during that window and move before the end date, not after.

sections.h2: What actually happens when a fixed deal ends
sections.html:
Nothing dramatic. Your supply is never cut off, you do not need to sign anything, and the lights stay on. What changes is the price. At midnight on the end date, your supplier moves you from the unit rates you fixed onto its standard variable tariff (SVT), the default tariff whose prices are limited by the Ofgem price cap and which the supplier can change every quarter when the cap moves.

Your supplier must warn you this is coming. Ofgem rules require an end-of-tariff notice between 42 and 49 days before your fix expires, telling you the end date, what tariff you will roll onto, and what the new rates are. It usually arrives by email and is easy to mistake for routine correspondence, so if your deal started roughly a year ago, go looking for it rather than waiting to notice it.

Two other things tend to happen around the same time:

  • Your direct debit may be recalculated. If the SVT rates are higher than your old fix, the supplier will usually raise your monthly payment to match, so check the new amount against your real annual usage.
  • Any credit balance stays with you. Rolling onto the SVT with the same supplier does not touch your account balance, and if you switch away, the old supplier must refund any credit after the final bill.

sections.h2: Your 49-day window: leave early with no exit fee
sections.html:
This is the single most useful rule to know. Ofgem bans suppliers from charging exit fees in the last 49 days of a fixed tariff. From the day that end-of-tariff notice can arrive, you are free to start a switch to any supplier, or to a different tariff with your current one, without paying the £25 to £75 per fuel that early exit usually costs.

The timing works in your favour. A switch normally completes in five working days, and if you start one inside the 49-day window your old supplier cannot apply exit fees even if the switch completes before the fix officially ends. You also get a 14-day cooling-off period after agreeing any new contract, so a quote you accept and then regret can be cancelled without cost.

The practical sequence looks like this:

  • Day 49 to about day 21: find your current rates and annual consumption (both are on your bill or in your supplier's app; our guide to understanding your energy bill shows where), then compare fixed quotes on a couple of comparison sites and directly with suppliers, since some deals are not listed on comparison sites.
  • By about day 14: start the switch or agree the new tariff. That leaves slack for the five working days plus any hiccups.
  • On the switch date: take meter readings, or check your smart meter sent them, so the closing bill is right.

If you miss the window entirely, nothing is lost except time. You can still switch from the SVT whenever you like, with no notice period and no exit fee, because default tariffs never carry one.

sections.h2: Work out what doing nothing would cost you
sections.html:
Before you can judge any new quote, you need the benchmark: what the standard variable tariff would cost you over a year. Here is the maths for a typical dual-fuel household using Ofgem's standard consumption figures of 2,700 kWh of electricity and 11,500 kWh of gas a year, paying by direct debit.

Cost lineCalculationCost per year
Electricity units2,700 kWh at 26.11p£705
Electricity standing charge57.19p per day for 365 days£209
Gas units11,500 kWh at 7.33p£843
Gas standing charge29.04p per day for 365 days£106
Typical dual-fuel total£1,863

At 26.11p per kWh electricity, 7.33p per kWh gas, standing charges of 57.19p and 29.04p per day. Ofgem price cap, July to September 2026, GB direct-debit averages. The cap changes every quarter, so check the current figures on Ofgem's website before you compare.

Swap in your own consumption from your annual statement and you have your personal benchmark. Then judge any fixed quote against it line by line, because small unit-rate differences add up over a year:

  • Every 1p per kWh off the electricity rate saves a typical home about £27 a year (more if you have an EV or electric heating).
  • Every 0.5p per kWh off the gas rate saves about £58 a year at typical use.
  • Every 5p per day off a standing charge saves about £18 a year per fuel. Standing charges vary between tariffs more than people expect, and they matter most in low-use homes.

Ignore the headline "£X per year" figure on comparison sites unless you have entered your real consumption, because it is otherwise based on typical use that may look nothing like yours.

sections.h2: Fix again, go variable, or pick a time-of-use tariff
sections.html:
Once you know your benchmark, there are three sensible destinations.

Fix again

A new fixed deal gives you a known price for 12 or 24 months, which makes budgeting easy and protects you if wholesale prices rise. The catch is the reverse: if the cap falls during your fix, you are stuck paying yesterday's price or paying an exit fee to leave. A fix priced below the current cap is worth a close look; a fix priced above it is a bet that prices will rise sharply, which you should only take deliberately. The full trade-off is covered in our guide to fixed versus variable tariffs.

Stay on the variable tariff

Rolling onto the SVT is not automatically a mistake. It carries no exit fee, so it is a reasonable holding position while you decide, and in a falling market it tracks prices down each quarter without you doing anything. Its weakness is the mirror image: it tracks prices up just as faithfully, with only a few weeks' notice each quarter.

Move to a time-of-use tariff

If you have an electric vehicle, a heat pump, storage heaters or a home battery, the end of a fix is the natural moment to look at time-of-use tariffs, which charge much less for electricity overnight. EV tariffs with off-peak rates around 7 to 9p per kWh, against 26.11p on the cap, change the economics of charging completely. Most need a working smart meter sending half-hourly readings, so check yours before you apply.

sections.h2: How to switch without a gap, a penalty or a billing mess
sections.html:
Switching is far less fragile than its reputation. The same wires and pipes serve your home regardless of supplier, so there is no interruption and nobody visits. The whole process is covered in detail in our guide to switching energy suppliers, but the short version for the end-of-fix moment is:

  • Apply with the new supplier, not the old one. The new supplier handles the whole transfer, including telling your current supplier you are leaving. You never need to ring up and cancel.
  • Expect five working days. Most switches now complete in that time. You can ask for a specific start date to line up with your fix ending.
  • Give meter readings on the switch day if you do not have a working smart meter, so the old supplier's final bill and the new supplier's first bill split your usage at the right point.
  • Keep paying your direct debit until the final bill arrives, usually within six weeks. Cancelling it early is how clean switches turn into debt-collection letters. Any remaining credit must then be refunded automatically.

One caveat: debt owed for more than 28 days can block a switch on a credit meter until it is cleared, and prepayment customers can only carry a limited debt across under the Debt Assignment Protocol. If that applies to you, agree a repayment plan with the supplier first.

A fixed deal ending is a deadline, not an emergency. Your supply continues either way; the only question is the price you pay from the end date onwards. Put the end date in your calendar when you take out any fix, watch for the notice six to seven weeks before it, and use the 49-day exit-fee-free window to compare properly. Benchmark every quote against the current cap using your own consumption, and start any switch a couple of weeks before the fix expires. The default outcome, rolling silently onto the standard variable tariff, costs a typical home about £1,863 a year at the July to September 2026 cap. Ten minutes of comparison either beats that or confirms you are already in the right place, and both are worth knowing.

faq.q: Will my energy be cut off when my fixed deal ends?
faq.a: No. Supply never stops when a tariff ends. You are moved automatically onto your supplier's standard variable tariff, priced under the Ofgem cap, and everything carries on as before except the unit rates and standing charges you pay.

faq.q: Can I switch before my fixed deal ends without paying exit fees?
faq.a: Yes, in the final 49 days of the fix. Ofgem rules ban exit fees in that window, so you can start a switch to any supplier or tariff as soon as your end-of-tariff notice arrives. Before the window opens, the exit fee stated in your contract applies, typically £25 to £75 per fuel.

faq.q: Is the price cap a limit on my total bill?
faq.a: No. The cap limits the unit rates and standing charges on default tariffs, not the total amount you pay. From July to September 2026 that is 26.11p per kWh for electricity and 7.33p for gas. Use more energy and you pay more; the often-quoted annual figure is just an illustration for a typical household.

faq.q: Should I wait until the deal has actually ended before switching?
faq.a: No, start during the last 49 days instead. Switches complete in about five working days, exit fees cannot be charged in that window, and starting early means you go straight from your fixed rates to the new tariff without spending weeks on the more expensive standard variable tariff in between.

faq.q: What happens to my credit balance and direct debit when the fix ends?
faq.a: Any credit stays on your account if you roll over with the same supplier, and is refunded after the final bill if you switch away. Your direct debit is usually recalculated for the new rates, so check the new monthly amount against your actual annual consumption rather than accepting it blind.
